Without PPSNI, prosecutions would stall, undermining rule of law, public safety, and property rights, leaving citizens vulnerable to unpunished crime and materially worse off.

items.name: Public Sector Fraud Authority
items.summary: The Public Sector Fraud Authority (PSFA) is a sub-organisation under the Cabinet Office (also linked to HM Treasury) with ~100 staff, working with departments and public bodies to understand and reduce the impact of fraud.
items.reason: Costs ~£7-10m annually for 100 staff plus overheads; functions can be absorbed into HM Treasury's existing audit, spending oversight, and risk management teams or handled via internal controls in individual departments, eliminating duplication; abolition would incentivise departments to prioritise fraud prevention directly (bearing costs themselves), close accountability gaps in this quasi-ALB, and avoid crowding out private-sector fraud detection firms that could bid competitively for departmental contracts.

items.name: Public Services Ombudsman for Wales
items.summary: Investigates complaints from the public about public service providers in Wales.
items.reason: Taxpayer-funded ALB with salaries, offices, and administrative costs diverting funds from productive uses; creates accountability gaps with minimal democratic oversight. Functions could cease or be absorbed into Welsh Government complaints teams or courts/private arbitration, as private sectors resolve disputes via reputation and litigation without such bodies; unseen consequences include institutional expansion incentives and crowding out market-based accountability.

items.name: Pubs Code Adjudicator
items.summary: The Pubs Code Adjudicator (PCA) enforces the Pubs Code, regulating tied pub agreements between large pub-owning companies and tenants, investigating complaints and arbitrating disputes under the Department for Business and Trade.
items.reason: Costs ~£1m+ annually in public funds for a niche regulator; functions like dispute resolution can be handled by private arbitration, courts, or market negotiations, reducing tenant/pubco imbalances naturally; abolition absorbs no essential role into DBT, eliminates accountability gaps and regulatory capture risks where industry lobbies influence rules, and frees resources from crowding out private contracts in a voluntary sector.

items.name: Race Equality Unit
items.summary: The Race Equality Unit (REU) is a sub-organisation of the Cabinet Office, working across government to identify and tackle racial and ethnic inequalities through legislation, policy, and data.
items.reason: Maintaining REU imposes costs on taxpayers for a bureaucracy that promotes social engineering rather than core government functions; its policy and legislative interventions distort markets and incentives, with salaries and operations funded by taxes diverted from productive uses. If abolished, functions could cease or be minimally absorbed into the Cabinet Office without loss of essential services, as addressing inequalities is better handled by private charity, civil society, and individual liberty. Unseen consequences include regulatory capture by activist interests, accountability gaps in a sub-unit with limited oversight, and crowding out of voluntary, market-driven solutions that foster genuine prosperity.

items.name: Rail Accident Investigation Branch
items.summary: The Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) is an independent body with ~43 staff that investigates rail accidents to improve safety and inform the industry and public, under the Department for Transport.
items.reason: RAIB provides essential independent accident investigations, free from conflicts of interest inherent in private rail operators self-regulating or DfT political influence, ensuring credible safety improvements that prevent future accidents, loss of life, and economic costs. Private sector cannot deliver impartial probes due to liability incentives; absorption into DfT would erode trust and accountability. With minimal headcount, citizens would be materially worse off without it via diminished rail safety oversight.

items.name: Rail Safety and Standards Board
items.summary: Independent rail industry body supporting members and stakeholders in delivering safer, more efficient, and sustainable rail operations.
items.reason: Costs taxpayers to maintain an independent quango with overlapping remits in safety standards and efficiency research that duplicate private sector capabilities (e.g., engineering firms, insurance-driven safety protocols); abolition would shift functions to rail operators' self-funded industry associations or absorb minimal oversight into DfT, reducing bureaucracy; unseen consequences include accountability gaps in spending public funds with little democratic oversight and crowding out private innovation in rail standards.

items.name: Regional Department for Education (DfE) Directors
items.summary: Sub-organisation of the Department for Education consisting of regional directors who work locally on children’s social care, SEND, schools, and area-based programmes to improve outcomes.
items.reason: This regional layer adds unnecessary bureaucracy and costs (director salaries, travel, offices) atop the already bloated DfE and local authorities; functions like oversight of social care, SEND, and schools are best devolved fully to elected local councils or existing DfE central teams, avoiding duplication with Ofsted inspections; abolition would save taxpayer money, reduce accountability gaps from diffused regional remits, and empower localism without material harm to outcomes, as private academies and voluntary providers already deliver efficiently where possible.

items.name: Regulatory Innovation Office
items.parentDept: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
items.summary: The Regulatory Innovation Office (RIO) is a sub-organisation of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) that aims to update regulations, speed up approval processes, and improve coordination among regulatory bodies to support innovation.
items.reason: Maintaining RIO incurs ongoing taxpayer costs for staff, operations, and overheads in a small but expanding bureaucracy that duplicates oversight functions already possible within DSIT or individual regulators. If abolished, its limited functions—updating regs, speeding approvals, and regulator coordination—could be absorbed directly into DSIT's core policy teams or devolved to regulators themselves, eliminating a layer of intermediation without loss of capability; true innovation thrives on deregulation, not bureaucratic streamlining. Unseen consequences include regulatory capture risks, where RIO becomes a tool for entrenched interests to influence rules, accountability gaps as a sub-body with diffused responsibility, and crowding out of private-sector innovation by perpetuating a regulatory state rather than shrinking it.

items.name: Remploy Pension Scheme Trustees Ltd
items.summary: Remploy Pension Scheme Trustees Ltd is a private company limited by guarantee that acts as trustee for the pension scheme of former Remploy Ltd employees (Disabled Persons’ Employment Corporation), with the Department for Work and Pensions as principal employer.
items.reason: This body fulfills a statutory requirement for independent trustees to administer an existing pension scheme, protecting entitlements of former disabled workers—a function private sector trustees perform but which requires separation from the employer (DWP) to avoid conflicts and ensure fiduciary duty. Abolishing it would risk legal challenges, mismanagement, or frozen benefits, leaving vulnerable pensioners materially worse off without viable absorption into DWP (which cannot self-trustee). Costs are minimal (typical trustee expenses), far outweighed by liability risks of disruption.

items.name: Research Collaboration Advice Team
items.parentDept: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
items.summary: RCAT provides advice to UK research institutions on national security risks associated with international research collaborations, operating as a sub-organisation within the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).
items.reason: National security in sensitive research areas requires government-held intelligence that private institutions lack access to; private sector or local alternatives cannot replicate this without creating vulnerabilities to espionage or tech transfer. Absorbed fully into DSIT already minimises bureaucracy; abolition would leave institutions exposed, increasing long-term risks to UK technological edge and prosperity without material cost savings given its focused, low-overhead role.

items.name: Research England
items.summary: Research England is a sub-organisation of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), responsible for funding and supporting research, knowledge exchange, and innovation in England's higher education institutions and research organisations.
items.reason: Maintaining Research England incurs significant costs as part of UKRI's £8 billion+ annual budget, funded by taxes on productive enterprise. Its functions—distributing grants and overseeing research—could cease entirely or be absorbed into a minimal core UKRI if needed, with universities and researchers turning to private funding from industry, philanthropists, and endowments, fostering competition and innovation. Unseen consequences include crowding out private R&D investment, regulatory capture by academic interests leading to safe, low-risk projects over bold innovation, and accountability gaps in an ALB with limited democratic oversight.

items.name: Reserve Forces' and Cadets' Associations (RFCA)
items.summary: Reserve Forces' and Cadets' Associations (RFCA) provide advice and assistance to the Defence Council and the armed forces on reserves and cadets matters, working with the Ministry of Defence.
items.reason: As an arms-length body, RFCA incurs separate administrative costs, duplicated back-office functions, and executive pay potentially exceeding private-sector equivalents, diverting funds from productive uses; its advisory role on reserves and cadets can be readily absorbed into the parent Ministry of Defence without loss of capability, eliminating accountability gaps and institutional incentives for expansion; abolition would reduce bureaucracy, prevent regulatory capture by reserve interests, and avoid crowding out private employer support for reserves.

items.name: Royal Marines Museum
items.summary: The Royal Marines Museum is a sub-organisation of the Ministry of Defence that preserves and exhibits the history of the Royal Marines through collections of over 1 million photographs, documents, and 30,000 objects.
items.reason: Running a specialised military history museum imposes ongoing public costs for curation, staffing, premises, and operations, diverting funds from productive enterprise. Its non-essential cultural functions could be absorbed by private regimental charities, ex-service associations, or larger institutions like the National Museum of the Royal Navy, eliminating duplication, accountability gaps, and bureaucratic expansion. Abolition would avoid crowding out private philanthropy in heritage preservation, with no material harm to citizens as private markets efficiently deliver museums via donations, tickets, and volunteers.

items.name: Royal Mint
items.summary: The Royal Mint, a Treasury-owned company over 1,100 years old, mints UK legal tender coins, produces commemorative coins, sells precious metal investments, jewellery, and recycles e-waste metals.
items.reason: Minting legal tender is a core sovereign function requiring state oversight to maintain monetary integrity, seigniorage profits (returned to Treasury), and national security in currency supply; private sector cannot replicate this monopoly without risking quality control, foreign dependence, or counterfeit vulnerabilities. Commercial activities (bullion, jewellery, recycling) are profitable and self-funding, not bloating the state. Britons would face unreliable coin supply, lost revenue, and diminished national symbolism without it; no better alternatives exist within existing departments.

items.name: Royal Navy Submarine Museum
items.summary: The Royal Navy Submarine Museum is a Ministry of Defence sub-organisation that preserves and displays the history of submarine development, focusing on the Royal Navy's Submarine Service, from early designs to modern nuclear vessels.
items.reason: Taxpayer-funded at an ongoing cost for curation, staffing, and maintenance, this non-operational museum duplicates functions that private charities, heritage trusts, or volunteers (e.g., submarine veterans' groups) could handle more efficiently via donations and admissions. Abolition would allow exhibits to be transferred to private or larger public museums like the National Museum of the Royal Navy, eliminating accountability gaps in a niche ALB while freeing funds for core MoD defence priorities; its existence crowds out private historical preservation and imposes unseen regulatory capture risks in grant allocations.

items.name: S4C
items.parentDept: Department for Culture, Media and Sport
items.summary: S4C is the publicly funded Welsh-language broadcaster, commissioning independent producers for programs in sport, drama, music, factual, entertainment, and events across platforms, under DCMS.
items.reason: Maintaining S4C costs taxpayers around £80m annually (plus license fee share), funding content with limited audience appeal that lacks commercial viability; abolition would cease operations, allowing private producers, subscriptions, or crowdfunding to meet demand if it exists, with functions unneeded by DCMS; unseen consequences include crowding out private Welsh media innovation, regulatory capture by commissioners, and accountability gaps as an ALB with minimal oversight.

items.name: Science and Technology Facilities Council
items.summary: The Science and Technology Facilities Council (STFC) manages national and international research facilities and campuses for fundamental research in astronomy, physics, computational science, and space science, as part of UK Research and Innovation.
items.reason: Large-scale facilities like synchrotrons, neutron sources, and telescopes demand enormous upfront capital and long-term funding with uncertain, non-excludable benefits, which the private sector systematically underprovides due to free-rider problems and lack of immediate profits; universities and existing departments lack the scale and coordination for such national assets. British citizens would be materially worse off without STFC, facing reduced scientific output, innovation spillovers to industry, brain drain of top talent, and diminished global standing in STEM.

items.name: Science Council
items.summary: The Science Council (SC) is an advisory body that works with the Food Standards Agency (FSA) to provide scientific input on food safety and standards.
items.reason: Maintaining a separate advisory council incurs unnecessary administrative costs, duplicated back-office functions, and accountability gaps typical of arms-length bodies; its functions can be absorbed into the FSA's existing scientific teams, contracted to private experts or universities, or handled ad-hoc, saving taxpayer funds without risking food safety. Unseen consequences include crowding out private-sector scientific innovation and potential regulatory capture by entrenched advisors.

items.name: Scientific Advisory Group for Emergencies
items.summary: SAGE provides scientific and technical advice to support government decision makers during emergencies.
items.reason: SAGE is an ad-hoc expert panel activated only during crises, incurring minimal ongoing costs unlike permanent quangos. Existing departments lack the rapid, cross-disciplinary scientific expertise it assembles; private sector consultants could theoretically advise but lack the pre-vetted, coordinated framework for national emergencies, risking delays or fragmented input. Without it, government decisions in crises (e.g., pandemics, disasters) would be poorer, materially harming citizens' safety, health, and economic stability.

items.name: Secret Intelligence Service
items.summary: The Secret Intelligence Service (MI6) collects foreign intelligence and provides covert capabilities to protect UK national security and economic interests, operating under the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office.
items.reason: Foreign intelligence and covert operations are core sovereign functions essential for national defense, which cannot be outsourced to the private sector due to risks of leaks, divided loyalties, and lack of legal authority; no existing department can replicate this specialised global capability. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing heightened vulnerabilities to foreign espionage, terrorism, cyber threats, and economic sabotage, undermining liberty and prosperity.

items.name: Sellafield Ltd
items.summary: Sellafield Ltd manages the decommissioning, cleanup, and safe management of the Sellafield nuclear site, one of the world's most hazardous nuclear facilities handling legacy waste, plutonium stocks, and contaminated infrastructure, under contract to the Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA).
items.reason: Nuclear decommissioning addresses a government-created legacy problem (nationalized nuclear industry) involving irreplaceable national security assets (e.g., plutonium) and extreme public safety risks that no private entity can assume without state-backed liability and oversight; private sector contractors operate under NDA contracts but cannot independently manage sovereign waste liabilities or externalities like radiation leaks affecting millions; without this body, cleanup halts, risking environmental catastrophe, health crises, and billions more in uncontrolled damages—far exceeding maintenance costs—while British citizens suffer direct harm absent this specialized function no other department can replicate.

items.name: Senior Courts Costs Office
items.summary: The Senior Courts Costs Office (SCCO) is a specialist court office within HM Courts & Tribunals Service that assesses and taxes legal costs in civil cases in the Senior Courts (High Court and Court of Appeal), determining recoverable costs between parties.
items.reason: This body performs a core judicial function integral to the operation of the court system: impartial assessment of legal costs to enforce judgments and prevent excessive billing. Privatisation risks inconsistency, bias, or capture by legal interests, undermining trust in public courts; existing departments lack the specialised expertise. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, as cost recovery would falter, deterring legitimate claims, prolonging disputes, and eroding access to justice.

items.name: Service Complaints Ombudsman
items.summary: Provides independent and impartial scrutiny of the handling of service complaints made by UK armed forces members, working with the Ministry of Defence.
items.reason: This arms-length body adds an unnecessary bureaucratic layer atop existing service-level complaints processes and judicial remedies like military courts or civilian judicial review. Its maintenance incurs taxpayer-funded costs for staff, operations, and executive pay, with institutional incentives to expand remit. Abolition would allow absorption into the MoD's internal governance or escalation to courts, eliminating accountability gaps in a quango with minimal democratic oversight, freeing funds for frontline defence, and avoiding crowding out of efficient private dispute resolution where applicable without harming service personnel rights.

items.name: Service Prosecuting Authority
items.summary: The Service Prosecuting Authority (SPA) is the principal prosecuting body within the UK's Service Justice System, handling prosecutions of service offences in Service Courts, under the Ministry of Defence.
items.reason: Prosecution in the military justice system requires specialised expertise in service law and independence from the chain of command to ensure fair trials, a core function not deliverable by the private sector (which lacks authority over military courts) or easily absorbed into the MoD without risking bias or reduced effectiveness. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, as undisciplined armed forces undermine national defence and security, a minimal-state essential.

items.name: South Downs National Park Authority
items.parentDept: Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs
items.summary: The South Downs National Park Authority manages the conservation, enhancement, and public enjoyment of the South Downs National Park, working under DEFRA.
items.reason: Maintaining this quango incurs significant costs (staff, offices, executive pay) with limited democratic oversight, creating accountability gaps typical of ALBs. Its planning and conservation functions could be absorbed by DEFRA or devolved to local authorities, reducing duplication across the UK's 15 national park authorities. Abolition would end regulatory capture by environmental interests, restore property rights to landowners, reduce barriers to rural development, and avoid crowding out private conservation efforts by charities or landowners who could preserve the area voluntarily if valued.

items.name: Sport Northern Ireland (Sports Council for Northern Ireland)
items.summary: Sport Northern Ireland (SportNI) is the leading public body for the development of sport in Northern Ireland, working with the Department for Communities.
items.reason: SportNI is an arms-length body incurring taxpayer costs for bureaucracy, executive pay, and grants, with limited democratic oversight. Its sports development functions—funding, facilities, and programmes—can cease or be absorbed into the parent Department for Communities with minimal loss, as private sports federations, sponsors, charities, and local clubs deliver these more efficiently via market incentives. Abolition avoids crowding out private investment, regulatory capture by sports lobbies, and accountability gaps, freeing funds for productive uses.

items.name: Sports Council for Wales
items.summary: Sports Council for Wales is the national organisation responsible for developing and promoting sport and physical activity in Wales, working with the Welsh Government.
items.reason: This ALB duplicates functions inherently provided by the private sector (sports clubs, gyms, leagues, sponsors) and voluntary organisations, with annual costs likely in the millions (staff, offices, grants) funded by taxes on productive enterprise. Abolition would end taxpayer subsidy, allowing market-driven sports promotion to flourish without government distortion; any residual coordination could cease or shift to local authorities/private entities, eliminating accountability gaps and preventing regulatory capture where the body lobbies for expanded remit.

items.name: Subsidy Advice Unit
items.summary: The Subsidy Advice Unit (SAU) is a sub-organisation of the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) that provides independent, non-binding advice to public authorities on compliance with the UK's subsidy control regime.
items.reason: Maintaining SAU incurs taxpayer costs for advising on subsidies, which distort markets and pick winners/losers, contradicting free-market principles. If abolished, public authorities could assess subsidies internally within parent departments or cease non-essential ones, eliminating duplication and reducing bureaucracy. Unseen consequences include legitimising market interventions, creating accountability gaps in subsidy decisions, and crowding out private enterprise by facilitating government spending over productive investment.

items.name: Technology and Construction Court
items.summary: The Technology and Construction Court is a specialist division of the High Court, handling complex disputes in technology, engineering, construction, and related fields, ensuring expert judicial resolution.
items.reason: Courts are a core state function essential for impartial dispute resolution, property rights enforcement, and contract upholding—foundations of markets and liberty that private arbitration cannot fully replace due to lack of universal enforceability and public oversight. This specialist court delivers efficiency and expertise not replicable by generalist courts or private entities without increasing delays and costs; abolishing it would overload HMCTS, deter investment in key growth sectors like construction and tech, and leave citizens worse off with slower, costlier justice.

items.name: The Adjudicator’s Office
items.summary: The Adjudicator’s Office investigates complaints against HMRC and VOA, and reviews Home Office Windrush Compensation Scheme decisions for fairness and consistency.
items.reason: Maintains a small but taxpayer-funded quango (est. low millions annually) with overlapping remits to existing mechanisms like HMRC internal reviews, independent tax tribunals, and the Parliamentary Ombudsman; abolition would absorb functions into HMRC or redirect to tribunals/Ombudsman, eliminating duplicated bureaucracy, reducing accountability gaps from arms-length status, and avoiding regulatory capture incentives without harming citizens, as fair recourse persists via established channels.

items.name: The Business and Property Courts
items.summary: The Business and Property Courts are specialist courts handling high-value commercial, property, and business disputes, operating across England and Wales as part of HM Courts & Tribunals Service.
items.reason: Courts are a core sovereign function essential for enforcing contracts, property rights, and rule of law—foundations of liberty, private property, and prosperity emphasized by Mises, Hayek, and Friedman. Private arbitration exists but lacks universal state enforcement power, accessibility for smaller parties, and uniform precedent; absorption into general courts would create backlogs harming business efficiency. British citizens and enterprises would be materially worse off without it, facing slower resolutions, reduced investment confidence, and weakened property protections, crowding out wealth creation.

items.name: The Business List
items.summary: The Business List is a specialist court list within the Business and Property Courts of the High Court, handling commercial disputes including company law, insolvency, and business contracts.
items.reason: This performs a core judicial function essential for enforcing contracts, property rights, and resolving commercial disputes impartially—functions requiring state monopoly on coercive enforcement that the private sector cannot replicate without undermining rule of law. Already integrated into HMCTS with no separate bureaucracy, staff expansion, or accountability gaps; British citizens and businesses would face delays, uncertainty, and economic harm without efficient specialist commercial courts.

items.name: The Competition List
items.summary: The Competition List is a specialist court list within the Chancery Division of the High Court, managed by HM Courts & Tribunals Service, handling competition law cases including appeals and enforcement actions.
items.reason: As a judicial function integrated into HMCTS, it enforces existing competition laws essential for rule of law and contract enforcement, which private arbitration cannot fully replace for statutory public interest cases; without it, complex competition disputes would overload general courts, delaying justice and harming market certainty—citizens materially worse off with inefficient generic handling.

items.name: The Crown Estate
items.summary: The Crown Estate is an independent commercial business managing a portfolio of UK Crown assets including buildings, shoreline, seabed, forestry, agriculture, and common land, generating £4.1 billion in net revenue to the Consolidated Fund over the last 10 years.
items.reason: While net revenue-positive (£410m/year), maintaining this state-owned entity incurs operating costs, executive pay exceeding private norms, and creates an accountability gap as an arms-length body with limited democratic oversight. Functions (asset management, leasing) could be fully privatized via asset sales/auctions, yielding a substantial one-off windfall to reduce national debt or cut taxes without ongoing state involvement; absorption into HM Treasury would politicize commercial decisions, risking inefficiency. Unseen consequences include market distortions from state monopoly (e.g., offshore wind leasing), crowding out private investment in land/seabed development, and institutional incentives for remit expansion beyond profit maximization.

items.name: The Executive Office (Northern Ireland)
items.summary: The Executive Office (Northern Ireland) provides support and coordination to the Northern Ireland Executive, the devolved power-sharing government.
items.reason: As a core executive support body for Northern Ireland's devolved administration under the Good Friday Agreement, it performs essential coordination functions that cannot be outsourced to the private sector or absorbed without undermining the unique power-sharing governance structure; without it, NI citizens would face governance instability, policy incoordination, and risks to political stability in a post-conflict region.

items.name: The Financial List
items.summary: The Financial List is a specialist jurisdiction within the High Court of England and Wales, part of HM Courts & Tribunals Service, handling complex, high-value financial market disputes including banking, insolvency, and market conduct cases to provide efficient expert adjudication.
items.reason: Courts are a core government function essential for enforcing contracts, property rights, and rule of law, which underpin markets and prosperity—functions inherently unsuited to private sector delivery due to needs for impartiality, enforceability, and public oversight. This specialized list enhances efficiency by assigning expert judges to financial cases, reducing delays and costs for litigants and the taxpayer compared to general lists; without it, complex cases would clog broader court backlogs, harming UK financial sector competitiveness and wealth creation, with no viable absorption into other departments as it is already integrated into the court service.

items.name: The Intellectual Property List
items.summary: Specialized court list within HM Courts & Tribunals Service handling intellectual property disputes.
items.reason: Performs essential judicial function of enforcing IP rights, vital for property rights, innovation, and market signals in a knowledge economy; private arbitration lacks state enforcement, and absorption into general courts would reduce efficiency via loss of specialist judges. Citizens materially worse off with slower, costlier IP justice, undermining UK competitiveness.

items.name: The Legal Ombudsman
items.summary: Independent scheme resolving complaints about lawyers in England and Wales, appointed by the Office for Legal Complaints to ensure fair resolution and improvements in legal services, working with the Ministry of Justice.
items.reason: Funded by levies on legal professionals (not direct taxpayers but still a regulatory burden diverting funds from productive use), this arms-length body creates an accountability gap with duplicated oversight; functions could cease or be absorbed into courts/private arbitration/professional self-regulation, reducing bureaucracy without harming consumers as market competition and civil courts already handle disputes effectively; unseen consequences include crowding out private dispute resolution, regulatory capture by the legal profession, and institutional incentives to expand remit at the expense of liberty and efficiency.

items.name: The National Shipbuilding Office
items.summary: The National Shipbuilding Office (NSO) is a government body established to develop and oversee the UK's national shipbuilding strategy, coordinating efforts across defence, commercial, and industrial policy to enhance domestic shipbuilding capabilities.
items.reason: Maintaining the NSO incurs ongoing costs in salaries, operations, and coordination (likely millions annually), diverting funds from productive private enterprise; its functions—strategy development and procurement oversight—could be absorbed into the Ministry of Defence (for naval needs) or Department for Business and Trade (for commercial), eliminating duplication; abolition would end accountability gaps, reduce regulatory capture by favoured contractors like BAE Systems, and prevent crowding out of private investment in maritime industries, allowing market-driven innovation without government picking winners.

items.name: The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman
items.summary: Investigates complaints about UK government departments and the NHS in England, operating independently and impartially.
items.reason: Costs taxpayers to maintain a dedicated quango with overlapping remits (NHS complaints could go to NHS England/DHSC, parliamentary to Cabinet Office or MPs); functions could be absorbed into parent departments with internal review processes or escalated to courts via judicial review, reducing duplication; creates accountability illusion as recommendations are non-binding, fostering regulatory capture by shielding departments from direct political/electoral scrutiny while evading democratic oversight; crowds out private arbitration or consumer-style dispute resolution in a freer market with less state involvement.

items.name: The Property, Trusts and Probate List
items.summary: Specialised court list within HM Courts & Tribunals Service handling disputes related to property, trusts, and probate.
items.reason: This performs core judicial functions enforcing property rights, trusts, and probate—essential for secure private property and contract enforcement, which Mises, Hayek, and Friedman viewed as prerequisites for wealth creation and individualism. Private arbitration cannot fully replace it, as it would exclude lower-income citizens and create accountability gaps; existing general courts lack specialisation, risking backlogs. Without it, Britons would face delays in inheritance, property transfers, and dispute resolution, materially harming economic confidence, liberty, and prosperity.

items.name: The Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee
items.summary: Ad-hoc advisory group under the Cabinet Office advising the Government and Royal Household on national memorialisation of Queen Elizabeth II.
items.reason: Costs ~£5-10m annually for ~100 staff on a temporary memorial project, creating unnecessary bureaucracy with accountability gaps typical of ad-hoc bodies; functions (advising on memorials) could be absorbed by the Cabinet Office, Royal Household, or private sector event planners without loss of output; unseen consequences include taxpayer funds diverted from productive uses, potential scope creep in remit, and crowding out of private commemorative initiatives.

items.name: The Revenue List
items.summary: The Revenue List is a specialist court list within HM Courts & Tribunals Service (HMCTS) that handles appeals and disputes related to tax and revenue matters, such as cases from HM Revenue & Customs.
items.reason: As a core component of the judicial system under HMCTS, it provides an essential, impartial forum for resolving tax disputes, enforcing rule of law and property rights critical to market functioning—functions inseparable from state authority that private arbitration cannot legitimately perform due to lack of enforcement power over government revenue decisions. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing unchecked HMRC discretion, eroded trust in tax administration, and higher compliance costs from legal uncertainty; it incurs negligible standalone costs as an integrated court list with no duplicated bureaucracy.

items.name: The Scottish Government
items.summary: Devolved executive government for Scotland, handling health, education, justice, rural affairs, transport, and most day-to-day issues, with 3 child organisations and 1 supporting public body.
items.reason: The Scottish Government represents a costly parallel bureaucratic structure duplicating core UK functions, with high maintenance expenses including salaries for ministers and civil servants equivalent to UK-wide roles, overlapping remits (e.g., separate health/education departments), and child bodies adding layers of expenditure—estimated at billions annually beyond what a unified UK system would require. Abolishing it would reintegrate functions into core UK ministerial departments (e.g., health into DHSC, education into DfE), enabling mergers, staff reductions, and economies of scale while local authorities or private providers handle delivery; unseen consequences include accountability gaps in devolved spending (less parliamentary oversight), regulatory capture by Scottish-specific interests driving higher per-capita spending that crowds out private investment, and institutional expansion unchecked by UK-wide fiscal discipline.

items.name: The Security Service
items.summary: MI5, the UK's domestic security service, protects against threats to national security such as terrorism and espionage, operating under the Home Office.
items.reason: National security intelligence cannot be effectively delivered by the private sector due to requirements for secrecy, legal powers (e.g., surveillance warrants), and coordination across borders; nor can it be absorbed into the Home Office without diluting operational independence. Without MI5, British citizens would face heightened risks of attacks, espionage, and subversion, materially undermining liberty, prosperity, and safety—no viable market alternative exists for this core state monopoly function.

items.name: Trade and Agriculture Commission
items.summary: Independent body that scrutinises signed free trade agreements (FTAs), particularly on trade and agriculture impacts, supported by a small DBT secretariat.
items.reason: Low but ongoing taxpayer cost for a small secretariat and operations; functions could be absorbed by DBT's existing trade policy teams or Parliament's International Trade Committee for pre- and post-signature scrutiny under CRaG, eliminating duplication. Unseen consequences include accountability gaps in a quango with minimal democratic oversight, potential for mission creep into broader trade interventions, and crowding out of private sector analysis by think tanks or trade bodies that could provide more dynamic, market-oriented critiques without public funding.

items.name: UK Asset Resolution Limited
items.summary: UKAR manages remaining liabilities and assets from the government's 2008 bailouts of Northern Rock Asset Management (NRAM), Bradford & Bingley (B&B), and Mortgage Express (MX), working with HM Treasury and UK Government Investments.
items.reason: UKAR is a legacy arms-length body from past interventions, sustaining bureaucracy, staff costs, and executive pay from taxpayer funds despite nearing wind-down. Functions like asset sales and liability management can be directly absorbed by HM Treasury or UKGI, closing accountability gaps and avoiding duplication. Its existence perpetuates sunk costs from moral-hazard bailouts, crowding out private asset managers; cessation poses no material risk to citizens as obligations diminish.

items.name: UK Commission on Covid Commemoration
items.summary: Temporary commission under Cabinet Office to develop consensus on national and community commemoration of COVID-19 pandemic, reporting to PM by March 2023.
items.reason: Non-essential, time-limited body with no core government function; commemoration can emerge organically via private initiatives, communities, or voluntary groups without taxpayer cost. Maintaining it incurs direct costs (staff, operations) and unseen opportunity costs (diverting resources from productive uses, precedent for proliferating ad-hoc commissions creating accountability gaps). Abolition results in no material harm—any needed memorials handled locally or privately—while saving public funds.

items.name: UK Council for Internet Safety
items.summary: The UK Council for Internet Safety (UKCIS) is an advisory forum collaborating with government, tech industry, and third sector to promote online safety, operating across Department for Education, Home Office, and DSIT.
items.reason: UKCIS is a non-essential talking shop with undisclosed but taxpayer-funded costs for staff, meetings, and operations; its advisory functions could cease entirely or be minimally absorbed into parent departments without loss, as online safety is better addressed by private tech innovations, parental responsibility, and voluntary civil society efforts; unseen consequences include accountability gaps in spending public money with minimal oversight, crowding out market-driven solutions, and risks of regulatory capture where big tech influences policy under guise of collaboration.

items.name: UK Defence and Security Exports
items.summary: UK Defence and Security Exports (UKDSE) is a sub-organisation of the Department for Business and Trade that promotes exports of UK defence, cyber, and security products and attracts overseas investment in the sector.
items.reason: Maintaining UKDSE incurs ongoing costs in staff, operations, and overheads, diverting taxpayer funds from productive uses; its promotional functions could be absorbed into the parent DBT's general trade services or handled entirely by private trade associations, consultants, and companies themselves, which already excel at export marketing without subsidy. Abolition would eliminate accountability gaps in deal-making, reduce cronyism risks where defence firms capture government favouritism, and avoid crowding out private export efforts, allowing market-driven trade to flourish unhindered.

items.name: UK Defence Innovation
items.summary: UK Defence Innovation (UKDI) is a sub-organisation of the Ministry of Defence aimed at achieving defence and security advantages through innovation.
items.reason: Maintaining a separate sub-organisation incurs unnecessary overhead costs, duplicated back-office functions, and executive pay premiums; its innovation functions can be fully absorbed into the core Ministry of Defence procurement and strategy teams or outsourced via competitive contracts to private defence firms like BAE Systems, which already drive military R&D more efficiently; unseen consequences include accountability gaps from its arms-length-like status, regulatory capture favouring connected contractors, and crowding out private sector investment in defence tech by diverting taxpayer funds from productive enterprise.

items.name: UK Government Investments
items.summary: UK Government Investments (UKGI) is the government’s centre of expertise in corporate finance and corporate governance, working with HM Treasury and supported by one public body, with three undefined child organisations.
items.reason: UKGI's maintenance imposes costs (staffing, operations) on taxpayers for expertise readily available in the private sector; government should not engage in corporate finance or hold stakes distorting markets. Residual functions (oversight of divestitures) could be absorbed into HM Treasury at lower cost, with full cessation as assets are privatized. Unseen consequences include crowding out private investors, regulatory capture favouring politically connected firms, and accountability gaps in opaque government deals.

items.name: UK Holocaust Memorial Foundation
items.parentDept: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
items.summary: Advises government on implementing recommendations of the Prime Minister’s Holocaust Commission, working with MHCLG.
items.reason: Costs ~£5-10m annually for 113 staff; advisory functions on Holocaust remembrance can be absorbed directly into MHCLG or DCMS with minimal staff; as an ALB creates accountability gap and risks mission creep/expansion; crowds out private charities and civil society efforts in Holocaust education/memorials, which deliver similar outcomes more efficiently without taxpayer funding.

items.name: UK Industrial Fusion Solutions
items.summary: UK Industrial Fusion Solutions (UKIFS) is a sub-organisation of the UK Atomic Energy Authority tasked with leading the STEP programme to design and build the UK's first prototype fusion energy plant.
items.reason: Maintaining UKIFS incurs massive costs (STEP budgeted at billions in taxpayer funds) for high-risk, long-term R&D that duplicates private-sector efforts by companies like Commonwealth Fusion Systems and UK startups; abolition would allow functions to cease or shift to private investors who better allocate capital without subsidies, freeing public money from productive enterprise while avoiding regulatory capture, accountability gaps in this quango-like body, and crowding out of market innovation—if abolished, fusion progress continues privately without government picking winners, preventing unseen waste from inevitable budget expansions.

items.name: UK Integrated Security Fund
items.summary: The UK Integrated Security Fund (UKISF) is a Cabinet Office sub-organisation providing a government-wide fund to address highest-priority national security threats at home and abroad.
items.reason: National security coordination is a core state function that cannot be delivered by the private sector due to its classified, collective-action nature and need for cross-departmental resource pooling. Siloed departmental budgets risk underfunding emergent, high-priority threats; UKISF enables flexible allocation via Cabinet Office oversight. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing delayed or inadequate responses to threats, undermining the secure environment essential for liberty, prosperity, and wealth creation. No evidence of bloat; costs likely minimal relative to risks.

items.name: UK National Authority for Counter-Eavesdropping
items.summary: UK National Authority for Counter-Eavesdropping (UK NACE) is a sub-organisation of FCDO Services providing technical security expertise against eavesdropping for government and critical national infrastructure (CNI), while leading the technical security profession across government.
items.reason: National security against espionage and eavesdropping is a core state function that private firms cannot uniformly deliver due to classified access requirements, conflicts of interest, and lack of mandatory oversight for all government departments and CNI. Existing departments lack this specialised expertise; absorption into FCDO or others risks dilution. Without UK NACE, vulnerabilities to foreign surveillance would rise, compromising sensitive operations, CNI resilience, and UK's geopolitical standing—materially harming liberty and prosperity via weakened security.

items.name: UK National Contact Point
items.summary: The UK National Contact Point (UK NCP) is a sub-organisation of the Department for Business and Trade responsible for promoting voluntary OECD guidelines on responsible business conduct for multinational enterprises and operating a complaints mechanism.
items.reason: This body promotes voluntary guidelines that businesses can follow without state intervention, and its complaints mechanism duplicates private arbitration, courts, or NGO dispute resolution. Costs include staff, operations, and overhead (even if small, taxpayer-funded), creating accountability gaps with minimal oversight. Abolition would absorb negligible functions into DBT or cease them entirely, freeing resources; unseen consequences include crowding out private mediators, enabling activist regulatory capture, and diverting focus from core trade promotion.

items.name: UK National Screening Committee
items.parentDept: Department of Health and Social Care
items.summary: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) advises ministers and the NHS across the UK on all aspects of population screening programmes and supports their implementation.
items.reason: Maintaining the UK NSC incurs overhead costs for staffing, operations, and coordination across devolved governments (est. millions annually in public funds), diverting resources from productive uses. Its advisory functions could be absorbed directly into the parent Department of Health and Social Care or NHS evidence teams without loss of capability, eliminating duplication with existing medical advisory bodies. Abolition would close accountability gaps in a quasi-independent structure, reduce regulatory capture by screening interests, and prevent crowding out of private-sector innovation in diagnostic technologies and personalized health services.

items.name: UK Resilience Academy
items.summary: The UK Resilience Academy (UKRA) is a sub-organisation of the Cabinet Office that provides learning and development training to government workers and those in the wider resilience sector.
items.reason: Maintaining UKRA incurs ongoing costs for staff, facilities, and operations, diverting taxpayer funds from productive uses; its training functions could be fully absorbed into the Cabinet Office's existing HR capabilities, outsourced to competitive private-sector providers (e.g., online platforms or specialist firms like those used by businesses), or delivered via local authorities, eliminating duplication and reducing headcount. Abolition would close an accountability gap in a non-essential quango-like body, preventing regulatory capture by training insiders seeking budget expansion, and avoid crowding out private training markets while imposing no material harm on resilience efforts, as core government functions like emergency response would continue uninterrupted.

items.name: UK Shared Business Services Ltd
items.parentDept: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
items.summary: UKSBS is a government-owned shared services provider offering HR, payroll, finance, procurement, and IT business services primarily to the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).
items.reason: UKSBS represents a non-core bureaucratic layer with high maintenance costs for duplicated back-office functions that private firms like Accenture or Capita deliver more efficiently at lower cost through competition and innovation. Abolishing it would enable DSIT to competitively outsource or absorb these services internally, slashing headcount, budgets, and executive pay; unseen consequences include crowding out private providers, regulatory capture in procurement, and accountability gaps as an arms-length body with minimal democratic oversight.

items.name: UK Visas and Immigration
items.summary: UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI) is a Home Office sub-organisation with ~7,500 staff responsible for millions of annual visa and entry decisions, prioritising national security and service for legal entrants.
items.reason: Border control and visa processing are core sovereign functions essential for national security, public safety, and controlling population inflows that impact wages, housing, and services; these cannot be delivered by the private sector (lacks enforcement monopoly) or fully absorbed elsewhere without capacity loss. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing uncontrolled immigration, heightened security risks, and economic strain from unchecked inflows. As a Home Office sub-unit, it avoids quango accountability gaps; high headcount reflects volume but merger into parent wouldn't yield major savings given specialised remit.

items.name: United Kingdom Reserve Forces Association
items.summary: The United Kingdom Reserve Forces Association (UKRFA) is a sub-organisation of the Ministry of Defence that promotes reserve forces efficiency through education, personal development, and international experiences.
items.reason: Maintaining UKRFA incurs costs for staff, administration, and operations that could be absorbed directly into the Ministry of Defence's existing reserve forces directorate, eliminating duplicated bureaucracy and accountability gaps inherent in sub-organisations. Its functions—education and development—can be delivered more efficiently by private training providers or MoD in-house programs, as seen in other militaries; ceasing the separate body would free taxpayer funds from productive enterprise without harming reserve readiness, avoiding regulatory capture by an expanding quango and crowding out private sector alternatives.

items.name: United Kingdom Security Vetting
items.summary: United Kingdom Security Vetting (UKSV) is the Cabinet Office's single government provider of National Security Vetting (NSV), serving as the centre of excellence for assessing and managing personnel security risks to protect citizens and enable vital public services.
items.reason: National security vetting requires centralized access to classified intelligence and uniform standards across government, which private sector firms cannot provide without risking leaks, conflicts of interest, or inconsistent application; existing departments lack the specialized expertise and would duplicate efforts if decentralized. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing heightened risks of espionage, sabotage, or insider threats in sensitive roles, compromising defence, intelligence, and critical infrastructure.

items.name: Veterans UK
items.summary: Veterans UK is a sub-organisation of the Ministry of Defence providing free support to veterans and families via helpline, welfare services, transition services, and injury/bereavement compensation payments.
items.reason: As a sub-organisation already within MoD, it likely incurs duplicated back-office costs and administrative overheads that could be eliminated by full absorption into the parent department. Compensation schemes fulfil specific contractual obligations from military service (injury/bereavement akin to insurance), but helpline, welfare, and transition services overlap with private charities (e.g., Royal British Legion) or could be market-delivered more efficiently; ceasing as separate entity avoids expansion incentives, accountability gaps, and taxpayer burden (every pound crowds out private support), with functions redirected to MoD core or voluntary sector without material harm to citizens.

items.name: Victims' Commissioner
items.summary: The Victims’ Commissioner promotes the interests of victims and witnesses, encourages good practice in their treatment, and reviews the Code of Practice for Victims, working with the Ministry of Justice.
items.reason: Maintaining this arms-length body incurs ongoing costs (executive salary, staff, offices) with minimal unique value; its core functions—reviewing the Victims' Code and promoting good practice—can be fully absorbed into the parent Ministry of Justice, which already oversees the justice system, victims' services, and code enforcement. Abolition closes an accountability gap, prevents institutional expansion and remit creep, and redirects funds from bureaucracy to productive uses or direct frontline support, without harming victims as MoJ retains oversight.

items.name: Wales Audit Office
items.summary: The Wales Audit Office (WAO) is an independent public body that audits public spending in Wales to ensure public bodies spend money wisely.
items.reason: The WAO imposes direct costs on taxpayers (staff, operations, executive pay likely exceeding private equivalents) to oversee other public spending, perpetuating bureaucracy with institutional incentives to expand its remit. Its core auditing functions could be fully contracted out to private firms like the Big Four, delivering efficient, competitive scrutiny without a permanent quango; the Welsh Government could tender contracts annually, absorbing minimal oversight into its finance team. Abolition would close the WAO's own accountability gap, prevent regulatory capture or remit creep, and avoid crowding out private audit expertise, redirecting savings to taxpayers while maintaining or improving audit quality through market discipline.

items.name: Welsh Government
items.summary: The Welsh Government is the devolved executive for Wales, led by the First Minister, responsible for key areas including health, education, and the environment, supported by 5 agencies and public bodies.
items.reason: The Welsh Government adds an extraneous layer of bureaucracy atop the UK state, with annual budgets exceeding £15 billion (mostly block grants from Westminster), thousands of civil servants, and overlapping remits with UK departments, fostering expansionist incentives, accountability gaps between Cardiff and Westminster, and regulatory capture in devolved sectors. Its functions—health, education, environment—could be privatized (e.g., market-based healthcare and schooling as in Friedman’s voucher models), devolved further to competitive local authorities, or recentralized into streamlined UK ministries for efficiency gains; abolition would eliminate duplicated back-offices, high executive pay, and crowd out private enterprise by taxing productive activity to sustain this parallel government, ultimately shrinking the state and boosting Welsh prosperity through liberty and markets.

items.name: Welsh Language Commissioner
items.summary: The Welsh Language Commissioner promotes and facilitates the use of the Welsh language in Wales, ensuring public services and interactions can be conducted in Welsh.
items.reason: Taxpayer-funded quango with overlapping cultural promotion remit that duplicates efforts possible via private NGOs, media, education, and voluntary societies; abolition would absorb any statutory duties into the Welsh Government, saving costs on bureaucracy and executive pay, while closing accountability gaps and preventing crowding out of organic, market-driven language preservation.

items.name: Windrush Commemoration Committee
items.parentDept: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
items.summary: A sub-organisation under the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, commissioned by the Prime Minister to advise on creating a permanent tribute to the Windrush generation.
items.reason: Minimal ongoing costs but still taxpayer-funded with accountability gaps typical of ad-hoc committees; functions are non-essential and symbolic, could be absorbed into MHCLG or handled by private charities, community groups, or cultural institutions without dedicated bureaucracy; perpetuates precedent for expanding state into commemoration, crowding out voluntary civil society efforts and diverting funds from productive uses.

items.name: Women and Equalities Unit
items.summary: The Women and Equalities Unit (WEU) is a sub-organisation of the Cabinet Office's Office for Equality and Opportunity, leading policy on gender equality, LGBT+ issues, and the UK's equality legislation framework.
items.reason: Maintaining this specialist unit incurs ongoing costs in staff, overheads, and budgets taxed from private enterprise, with duplicated efforts across departments. Functions could be absorbed into core Cabinet Office policy teams or line departments like Justice (for legislation) without loss of efficacy; equality laws would persist, and private sector incentives would drive genuine equality better than state mandates. Unseen consequences include accountability gaps in niche advocacy, regulatory capture by interest groups, and scope creep into social engineering that crowds out market-based individualism.

items.name: Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority
items.parentDept: Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs
items.summary: The Yorkshire Dales National Park Authority (YDNP) is an arms-length body under DEFRA responsible for conserving and enhancing the natural beauty, wildlife, and cultural heritage of the Yorkshire Dales, while promoting public understanding and enjoyment.
items.reason: Maintaining a separate quango like YDNP incurs significant costs in staff, administration, and executive pay, duplicating functions across 15 UK national park authorities; planning, conservation enforcement, and tourism promotion could be absorbed by local councils (already involved in land-use planning) or DEFRA directly, eliminating accountability gaps and overlapping remits; abolition would end regulatory capture by park bureaucracies expanding budgets, allow private landowners and charities (e.g., National Trust) to drive market-led conservation and tourism via property rights incentives, and free up taxpayer funds without harming public access, as legal designations persist.

items.name: Youth Custody Service
items.summary: Youth Custody Service (YCS) is a sub-organisation of HM Prison and Probation Service, responsible for providing safe, decent, and nurturing custodial environments with high levels of care and support for children in custody.
items.reason: As a sub-organisation, YCS creates unnecessary administrative layers, duplicated back-office functions, and higher overhead costs within the already bloated HMPPS structure, diverting taxpayer funds from productive uses. Abolishing it would absorb its core custodial functions directly into HMPPS, eliminating these inefficiencies and accountability gaps without loss of service delivery, as youth custody remains a necessary state monopoly function best streamlined under a single parent body rather than siloed.

items.name: Academy for Social Justice
items.summary: The Academy for Social Justice is a learning network under the Ministry of Justice, targeting people in England and Wales interested in improving public services and promoting social thriving, with ~14,000 staff.
items.reason: Maintaining this body incurs massive costs from ~14,000 staff, equivalent to a major department's payroll, diverting funds from productive enterprise; its training and networking functions could be fully privatized via universities, online platforms, or MoJ internal programs with no loss in effectiveness and lower expense; abolition would eliminate an accountability gap in this arm's-length body, prevent regulatory capture by 'social justice' advocates expanding its remit, and avoid crowding out private-sector education providers while refocusing MoJ on core justice delivery.

items.name: Accelerated Capability Environment
items.summary: Accelerated Capability Environment (ACE) is a Home Office sub-organisation focused on innovative approaches to public sector technology and data challenges.
items.reason: ACE duplicates functions that the private sector excels at, such as tech innovation and data solutions, which could be procured via competitive tenders at lower cost and higher efficiency; maintaining it incurs overhead costs (staff, operations) funded by taxation, crowding out private investment in tech; abolition would allow absorption of any critical Home Office needs into the parent department or outsourcing, eliminating accountability gaps in a niche unit prone to mission creep without democratic oversight.

items.name: Administration of Radioactive Substances Advisory Committee
items.parentDept: Department of Health and Social Care
items.summary: ARSAC is an advisory committee that provides guidance to licensing authorities on applications for using radioactive substances on humans, working under the Department of Health and Social Care.
items.reason: As a small advisory quango, ARSAC incurs administrative costs (staff, meetings, oversight) that divert funds from productive uses; its niche advisory function on medical radiation licensing could be fully absorbed into the parent DHSC or MHRA without loss of expertise or safety, eliminating accountability gaps, duplicated efforts, and institutional bloat while maintaining regulation via existing core bodies—no evidence citizens would be worse off, as licensing authorities would continue operations seamlessly.

items.name: Administrative Court
items.summary: The Administrative Court is a specialist division of the High Court, handling judicial reviews, appeals, and claims against public authorities under HM Courts & Tribunals Service.
items.reason: This court performs an essential rule-of-law function—judicial oversight of government and public body actions—that cannot be delivered by the private sector (due to need for impartial state authority) or absorbed into existing departments without undermining judicial independence. Without it, British citizens would be materially worse off, facing unchecked bureaucratic overreach, reduced liberty, and no recourse against arbitrary state decisions, contradicting core principles of limited government with accountability.

items.name: Admiralty Court
items.summary: The Admiralty Court is a specialist division of the High Court within HM Courts & Tribunals Service, handling maritime disputes including ship arrests, collisions, salvage, and cargo claims.
items.reason: This court performs an essential judicial function enforcing property rights and contracts in the vital maritime sector, which underpins UK trade, insurance (Lloyd's of London), and global shipping. Private arbitration handles many commercial maritime disputes but lacks statutory coercive powers like in rem ship arrests in UK ports, unique to Admiralty jurisdiction. Absorbing into a general court would dilute expertise; private sector cannot replace state-enforced adjudication. Without it, UK would lose competitiveness as a maritime hub, harming prosperity; costs are justified as minimal for rule-of-law infrastructure supporting liberty and commerce.

items.name: Advisory Committee for Social Science
items.summary: The Advisory Committee for Social Science (ACSS) is an advisory body under the Food Standards Agency (FSA), providing social science input on food standards and policy.
items.reason: ACSS represents classic quango bloat: an unnecessary advisory layer with unquantified but taxpayer-funded costs for meetings, members' fees, and administration, duplicating expertise available within FSA or via private consultants. Abolition would see any social science advice absorbed directly into FSA's core team or outsourced on-demand, eliminating accountability gaps, regulatory capture risks in niche consultations, and crowding out of private-sector social research firms; no material harm to food standards, as core safety functions remain intact.

items.name: Advisory Group on Military and Emergency Response Medicine
items.summary: Advisory Group on Military and Emergency Response Medicine (AGMERM) provides specialist medical advice to the Ministry of Defence on defence against chemical, biological, and radiological threats.
items.reason: National defence against CBRN threats is a core state monopoly function requiring classified, specialist medical expertise that private sector cannot provide due to security clearances and lack of incentive; existing MoD capabilities benefit from external advisory input to avoid groupthink; British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing heightened vulnerability to catastrophic threats with no viable alternative delivery mechanism.

items.name: Air Accidents Investigation Branch
items.summary: The Air Accidents Investigation Branch (AAIB) investigates civil aircraft accidents and serious incidents in the UK, its overseas territories, and crown dependencies, working with the Department for Transport to improve aviation safety.
items.reason: AAIB performs an essential independent safety investigation function mandated by international obligations (e.g., ICAO Annex 13), which cannot be reliably delivered by the private sector due to inherent conflicts of interest among airlines, insurers, or manufacturers that could bias findings and undermine safety improvements. Absorbing into DfT risks political interference and reduced credibility. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing higher aviation accident risks, loss of life, economic disruptions from unsafe skies, and potential international sanctions or loss of aviation market access.

items.name: AI Security Institute
items.parentDept: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
items.summary: The AI Security Institute (AISI) is a sub-organisation of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), tasked with researching advanced AI risks, developing mitigations, and equipping governments with scientific understanding.
items.reason: AISI imposes costs on taxpayers for research and operations that duplicate private sector efforts by firms like Anthropic and OpenAI safety teams, or academia; functions could be absorbed into DSIT's existing policy teams or cease without harm, as market-driven innovation better assesses and mitigates AI risks. Unseen consequences include regulatory capture biasing standards against competition, accountability gaps in an arms-length body, and crowding out UK private AI leadership.

items.name: Arts and Humanities Research Council
items.summary: The Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC) is a sub-organisation of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) that funds research in arts and humanities subjects.
items.reason: AHRC represents non-essential taxpayer spending on research that the private sector—universities via tuition fees, philanthropy, corporate sponsorships, and endowments—already funds effectively, with market signals determining value rather than bureaucratic allocation. Maintaining AHRC costs millions annually in grants and overheads (part of UKRI's £8bn+ budget), creating accountability gaps as an arms-length body with limited democratic oversight and risks of academic regulatory capture. Abolishing it would allow functions to cease or shift to competitive private/university funding, freeing resources for productive enterprise without harming prosperity; unseen consequences include crowding out private innovation and perpetuating dependency on state grants.

items.name: Arts Council of Northern Ireland
items.summary: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland (ACNI) is the development and funding agency for the arts in Northern Ireland, working with the Department for Communities.
items.reason: Costs millions in taxpayer funds annually to subsidise arts, diverting resources from productive enterprise. Abolition would cease state funding, with arts thriving via private markets, ticket sales, patrons, and philanthropy—no absorption needed as this is non-essential. Creates accountability gap as an ALB, enables regulatory capture by arts lobby, and crowds out voluntary private support.

items.name: Arts Council of Wales
items.summary: The Arts Council of Wales is an arms-length body established by Royal Charter in 1994, working with the Welsh Government to support and fund arts and culture in Wales.
items.reason: As a non-essential ALB, it spends millions in taxpayer funds annually on grants and administration, creating accountability gaps with minimal democratic oversight and high executive pay. Abolishing it would end state subsidy for arts, allowing the private sector (via sponsorships, philanthropy, ticket sales) and voluntary organisations to fill any gaps more efficiently, without crowding out market-driven cultural innovation or fostering dependency and regulatory capture by arts lobbies. Functions could cease entirely or be minimally absorbed into Welsh Government cultural policy if needed, freeing funds for productive enterprise.

items.name: Bank of England
items.summary: The Bank of England is the United Kingdom's central bank, responsible for setting monetary policy, issuing banknotes, acting as lender of last resort, and promoting financial stability.
items.reason: Maintaining the Bank costs over £1 billion annually in operational expenses, staff (c.4,500 employees), and executive pay exceeding private-sector equivalents, funded by seigniorage and taxpayer backstops. Its functions—discretionary monetary policy, note issuance, and stability oversight—could cease or be absorbed into HM Treasury for rule-based policy (e.g., Friedman's k-percent rule) or devolved to private banks via free banking as advocated by Hayek and Mises, eliminating boom-bust cycles from fiat manipulation. Unseen consequences include enabling deficit monetization, moral hazard crowding out sound private finance, regulatory capture by City interests, and accountability gaps from operational independence shielding poor decisions like quantitative easing from democratic scrutiny.

items.name: BBC World Service
items.summary: BBC’s international broadcaster providing news in 42 languages to global audiences, funded by FCDO with objectives set by Foreign Secretary.
items.reason: Funded by taxpayer grant (~£285m/year), creating accountability gap via Foreign Secretary oversight risking politicised 'impartial' news. Private broadcasters (CNNi, Reuters) already serve global audiences efficiently without subsidy; abolition saves costs, ends market distortion/crowding out of commercial media, and avoids regulatory capture in soft power propaganda.

items.name: Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council
items.summary: The Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC) is a research council under UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) that funds research in biotechnology and biological sciences to advance biology for health, prosperity, and sustainability.
items.reason: BBSRC incurs substantial administrative and overhead costs (part of UKRI's £8bn+ annual budget), with duplicated back-office functions across UKRI's councils. Its grant-funding role could be absorbed into a streamlined UKRI or ceased entirely, allowing universities and private firms (e.g., pharma giants like GSK, AstraZeneca) to fund R&D via market incentives. Abolition avoids crowding out private investment, prevents regulatory capture by state-favored researchers, and closes accountability gaps in opaque grant allocation, redirecting taxpayer funds to productive enterprise.

items.name: Bona Vacantia
items.summary: Bona Vacantia Division (BVD) administers ownerless property (bona vacantia) passing to the Crown in England and Wales, including estates of intestate individuals without kin and assets of dissolved companies, as part of the Government Legal Department.
items.reason: This performs a core statutory legal function enforcing property law where assets revert to the Crown by sovereign right, which cannot be delivered by the private sector without fundamental legal reform; already efficiently integrated as a sub-organisation of the Government Legal Department with minimal overhead and no evidence of bloat, duplication, or accountability gaps; abolition would leave assets uncollected, reducing Treasury revenue without serving public interest, and British citizens would be worse off with foregone public funds from legitimate escheats.

items.name: Border Force
items.summary: Border Force is a law enforcement command within the Home Office responsible for securing UK borders through immigration and customs controls on people and goods entering the UK.
items.reason: Border control is a core sovereign function essential for national security, preventing illegal immigration, terrorism, smuggling, and crime; this cannot be reliably delivered by the private sector due to risks of corruption, inconsistent enforcement, and lack of democratic accountability, nor fully absorbed into the parent Home Office without losing operational effectiveness. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing unsecured borders, increased security threats, and economic costs from uncontrolled inflows and illicit trade.

items.name: Border Security Command
items.summary: Border Security Command (BSC) is a Home Office sub-organisation providing strategic leadership to disrupt organised crime groups facilitating irregular migration, such as small boat crossings.
items.reason: Maintaining a separate command structure incurs duplicated overheads, staff, and budgets that could be absorbed directly into the Home Office or existing agencies like Border Force and National Crime Agency (NCA), which already handle border enforcement and organised crime; this eliminates bureaucratic layers, reduces accountability gaps from fragmented leadership, and avoids crowding out frontline resources, with no material loss in border security as core functions persist under streamlined oversight.

items.name: British Business Bank
items.summary: Government-owned development bank providing loans, equity investments, and guarantees to UK businesses, especially SMEs, where private finance is deemed insufficient.
items.reason: The BBB costs taxpayers over £100m annually in operations plus risks of loan losses and impairments exceeding £1bn historically. Its functions should cease entirely, as private banks and markets would allocate capital more efficiently through price signals and risk assessment, without government distortion. Any residual policy needs could be handled by DBT directly. Unseen consequences include moral hazard (businesses taking undue risks expecting bailouts), crowding out private lenders, regulatory capture by favoured sectors, and accountability gaps in an ALB managing £30bn+ in assets.

items.name: British Cattle Movement Service
items.summary: The British Cattle Movement Service (BCMS) maintains the Cattle Tracing System (CTS), an online database tracking all bovine animals in Great Britain, as a sub-organisation of the Rural Payments Agency.
items.reason: BCMS performs a critical biosecurity and traceability function essential for preventing and controlling animal disease outbreaks (e.g., foot-and-mouth), which protects the £14bn UK livestock sector and public health. The private sector cannot deliver this as it requires mandatory national reporting, enforcement, and a centralized public database—farmers would not fund or comply voluntarily, leading to fragmented data and slower responses. Existing departments like DEFRA/RPA handle it efficiently without duplication. British citizens would be materially worse off without it, facing massive economic losses from uncontained epidemics, export bans, and taxpayer-funded mass culls.

items.name: British Technology Investments Ltd
items.parentDept: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
items.summary: British Technology Investments Ltd invests public funds in advanced technology firms through the National Security Strategic Investment Fund (NSSIF), working with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.
items.reason: Costs include administrative overheads, executive pay, and risk of taxpayer losses on poorly-chosen investments. Abolishing it would cease government venture capital activities, freeing funds for private enterprise; private VCs have superior incentives to identify winners in security tech without distorting markets. Unseen consequences: crowding out private investment, regulatory capture by favoured firms, and accountability gaps in an arms-length body spending public money with limited oversight.

items.name: British Wool
items.parentDept: Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs
items.summary: British Wool is a body that collects, grades, promotes, and markets British wool on behalf of sheep farmers to boost demand and returns, working with Defra.
